question: The government should be allowed to take land from its owners without paying them, in order to fix the inequalities caused by apartheid.
context: A new law (the Expropriation Act, 2025) allows the government to take privately owned land without paying the owner in certain situations. Supporters say this is the only way to fix the land inequality left behind by apartheid. Critics say it is unfair to property owners and will scare away investors and businesses.

question: Private companies should be allowed to compete freely in producing and selling electricity, with Eskom's role greatly reduced.
context: Right now, Eskom controls most of South Africa's electricity supply. There is a debate about whether private companies should be allowed to produce and sell electricity in open competition with Eskom, similar to how there are multiple competing cellphone networks, or whether Eskom should remain the main supplier with only limited private involvement.

question: The National Health Insurance (NHI) should be fully implemented, replacing private medical aids as the main way healthcare is paid for in South Africa.
context: The NHI is a government plan to create one national fund that pays for healthcare for all South Africans, regardless of income. Private medical aids would be limited to covering services the NHI does not cover. Supporters say it is the only way to give everyone access to good healthcare. Critics say the government cannot afford it, that the state does not have the capacity to run it properly, and that parts of it may be unconstitutional. Several court cases are currently challenging the law.

question: South Africa needs significantly more police officers and harsher sentencing for violent crime.
context: South Africa has one of the highest murder rates in the world. There is ongoing debate about whether the solution is hiring more police officers and giving courts the power to hand down longer sentences, or whether the root causes of crime, such as poverty and unemployment, need to be addressed first. Many argue both approaches are needed at the same time.

question: Race-based economic empowerment policies like BBBEE should remain a central tool for addressing inequality.
context: Black Economic Empowerment (BEE) is a set of government policies designed to increase Black ownership of businesses, representation in management, and access to government contracts. Supporters say these policies are necessary to correct the economic exclusion caused by apartheid. Critics, including some Black economists, argue that the benefits mostly go to a small, well-connected elite, and that policies based on financial need rather than race would be more fair and effective.

question: South Africa should enforce significantly stricter controls on immigration and reduce the number of migrants permitted to settle.
context: South Africa is home to an estimated 2 to 4 million undocumented migrants, and violence targeting foreign nationals has been a recurring problem. Some argue that stricter border controls are needed because migrants compete with locals for jobs and put pressure on schools, hospitals, and other services. Others argue that migrants contribute to the economy and that South Africa has signed international agreements obligating it to protect refugees and asylum seekers.

question: The government should fully cover the cost of university and college fees for all students who qualify academically.
context: The #FeesMustFall protests in 2015 and 2016 pushed the government to expand financial aid for students through NSFAS, a scheme that covers fees for students from poorer households. The debate now is whether this should be extended so that the state covers fees for all academically qualifying students, regardless of income. Supporters say education is a right and fees are a barrier. Critics argue the government cannot afford it and that the money would be better spent fixing struggling schools and colleges first.

question: South Africa should align itself more closely with Western countries like the USA and EU, even if it strains our relationships with Russia and China.
context: South Africa is a member of BRICS, a group of major emerging economies that includes Brazil, Russia, India, China, and South Africa. In recent years, SA has abstained from UN votes condemning Russia's invasion of Ukraine, conducted joint military exercises with Russia and China, and faced allegations of supplying arms to Russia. Some argue SA should distance itself from authoritarian governments and align more closely with Western democracies. Others argue SA should remain independent and neutral, prioritising its own economic interests and relationships across the Global South.

question: Government-owned companies like Transnet and South African Airways should remain under state ownership rather than being sold to private investors.
context: South Africa owns several large companies that provide essential services, including Transnet, which runs railways and ports, Eskom, which generates electricity, and South African Airways. Many of these have needed billions of rands in government bailouts due to mismanagement and corruption. Some argue they should be sold or partially handed over to private investors to improve performance and stop draining public funds. Others argue that selling them would put essential services in private hands and harm workers and communities who depend on them.

question: The government should do far more to tackle gender-based violence, including harsher sentences for perpetrators and better support for survivors.
context: South Africa has one of the highest rates of gender-based violence in the world. More than 10,000 sexual offences are reported to police every quarter, and femicide rates are among the highest globally. The government declared GBV a second pandemic in 2020 and introduced a National Strategic Plan, but many argue implementation has been slow and underfunded. Debate exists between those who want harsher criminal sentences and a stronger police response, and those who argue the root causes, including poverty, inequality, and harmful cultural attitudes, must be addressed at the same time.

question: Abortion should remain legal and be available free of charge through the public health system.
context: South Africa has had one of the most progressive abortion laws in the world since 1996, allowing any woman to choose to end a pregnancy in the first 12 weeks without needing to give a reason. The debate today is about whether this right should be actively protected and made more accessible through public clinics, or whether it should be restricted on moral, religious, or budgetary grounds. Some parties oppose abortion on religious principles.

question: The rights of LGBTQ+ people, including the right to same-sex marriage, should be protected and strengthened.
context: South Africa was the first country in Africa and one of the first in the world to legalise same-sex marriage, in 2006. The Constitution explicitly protects people from discrimination based on sexual orientation. However, LGBTQ+ people still face significant discrimination and violence in practice. Some political parties actively oppose LGBTQ+ rights on religious or cultural grounds, while others support expanding protections and improving enforcement.

question: The government should run dedicated programmes, such as subsidised jobs and apprenticeships, specifically targeting the millions of young South Africans who are unemployed.
context: More than 60% of young South Africans are unemployed, one of the highest rates in the world. Millions more are not in any job, education, or training programme. Some argue the government needs to directly create opportunities for young people through subsidised employment and expanded learnerships. Others argue the real solution is making it easier for businesses to hire, so the private sector can create sustainable jobs.

question: Government jobs and contracts should be awarded based on ability and fair competition, not on political connections or party loyalty.
context: A practice known as cadre deployment involves political parties, particularly the ANC, placing party loyalists in senior government and state-company positions regardless of their qualifications. Critics say this is a major cause of corruption and the collapse of service delivery in many areas. Supporters argue it ensures that the people running the state are ideologically committed to transformation. Almost all opposition parties support ending the practice.

question: Improving South Africa's schools requires more government spending, not only stricter accountability for teachers and principals.
context: South Africa consistently scores near the bottom in international tests of reading and mathematics, and roughly half of children who start Grade 1 never reach matric. Many schools, particularly in rural and township areas, lack basic infrastructure like libraries, laboratories, and reliable electricity. Some argue the primary solution is increased funding to fix these inequalities in resources. Others argue that money alone is not enough and that stronger accountability, including performance assessments for teachers and consequences for underperforming schools, is equally necessary.

question: The government should prioritise upgrading townships and informal settlements by investing in housing, roads, electricity, and sanitation in these areas.
context: Millions of South Africans live in townships and informal settlements that were created or neglected under apartheid, many still without reliable access to clean water, proper sanitation, paved roads, or decent housing. The government's RDP and BNG housing programmes have built millions of homes but are widely seen as insufficient and slow. Debate exists between those who argue the state must massively increase direct investment in upgrading these communities, and those who argue the focus should be on growing the broader economy so that people can afford to move out of poverty themselves.

question: When a municipality is failing to provide water, electricity, or sanitation, private companies should be brought in to run those services.
context: More than 60 municipalities across South Africa are in serious financial trouble and unable to reliably deliver basic services. Service delivery protests happen hundreds of times a year. Some argue that bringing in private companies to manage failing services would improve efficiency and accountability. Others argue that private companies put profit before people, tend to raise prices, and that the solution is better-run government rather than handing services over to the private sector.

question: Fixing broken government services like hospitals, Home Affairs, and SASSA requires more than money — it requires overhauling the way they are run.
context: Many South Africans experience government services as slow, unreliable, and often corrupt. Hospitals run short of medicine, Home Affairs offices have long queues, and SASSA grants are sometimes delayed. There is debate about the cause. Some say the problem is a lack of funding and argue more investment is needed. Others say the deeper issue is poor management, corruption, and hiring people based on politics rather than skill, and that structural reform is what is required.

question: The government should play an active role in directly creating jobs through public works programmes and incentives for businesses to hire.
context: South Africa's official unemployment rate is around 32 to 33%, one of the highest in the world. Some argue the government needs to step in directly by funding large-scale public employment programmes and offering incentives to get businesses to hire more workers. Others argue the best way to create lasting jobs is to reduce regulations, lower taxes, and make it easier to do business, so the private sector can grow and create jobs on its own.

question: South Africa's labour laws should be relaxed to make it easier for businesses to hire and fire workers, in order to reduce unemployment.
context: South Africa has some of the most protective labour laws in the world, making it legally difficult and expensive for employers to dismiss workers. Business groups argue these laws discourage companies from hiring, especially young and first-time workers. Trade unions and left-leaning parties argue that weakening these laws would take away job security and push wages down for already vulnerable workers. Parties like the DA, ActionSA, and BOSA support relaxing these laws, while unions and the EFF strongly oppose it.

question: The government should actively protect South African factories and manufacturers by requiring local purchasing and taxing cheaper imported goods.
context: South Africa's manufacturing sector has shrunk significantly over the past four decades, and deindustrialisation has cost the country millions of jobs. Policy options being debated include requiring government departments to buy locally made goods, providing support to key industries like clothing, steel, and cars, and placing tariffs (taxes) on imported goods that compete with local products. Supporters say this protects jobs and builds the economy. Critics say it makes goods more expensive for consumers and can trigger trade disputes with other countries.

question: Mining companies should be more strictly regulated and taxed, so that local communities and the environment benefit more from South Africa's mineral wealth.
context: Mining contributes about 8% of South Africa's economy and employs hundreds of thousands of workers. However, many communities near mines see little of the wealth, and environmental damage is a recurring concern. Debate exists about whether stronger taxes and regulations on mining companies would better compensate affected communities and protect the environment, or whether stricter rules would push mining investment to other countries and cost jobs.

question: South Africa should move as quickly as possible to solar and wind energy and reduce its reliance on coal.
context: About 85% of South Africa's electricity currently comes from coal-fired power stations. A major international funding deal worth over R130 billion aims to help South Africa shift towards renewable energy like solar and wind, while supporting the roughly 90,000 workers whose jobs depend on coal, mostly in Mpumalanga. Supporters of the shift say coal is destroying the environment and that renewables are now cheaper. Critics argue that moving too fast risks energy shortages and threatens coal workers' livelihoods before alternative jobs are in place.

question: The government should create a state-owned bank and tighten regulations on private banks to ensure more South Africans can access loans and financial services.
context: South Africa's five largest private banks control more than 90% of all banking. An estimated 11 million South African adults do not have proper access to banking or credit. A state bank has been proposed, with the Post Office's Post Bank often discussed as a vehicle for this, though it has struggled with serious governance problems. Those in favour argue a public bank would serve poorer communities that private banks ignore. Those against argue the solution is more competition between banks and better use of technology, not a new state institution.

question: South Africa should work towards closer economic and political ties with other African countries, even if it means making it easier for people from those countries to live and work here.
context: A major continental trade agreement, the African Continental Free Trade Area, aims to make it easier to trade and move between African countries. Some parties argue South Africa should embrace deeper African integration as both an economic opportunity and a moral obligation to the continent. Others argue that with unemployment and service pressure as high as they are, South Africa cannot afford to open its borders further, and that freer movement would worsen competition for jobs and strain public services.
